root_text.sn22.150:1.2: “Kismiṁ nu kho, bhikkhave, sati, kiṁ upādāya uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhan”ti?
root_text.sn22.150:1.4: “rūpe kho, bhikkhave, sati rūpaṁ upādāya uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn22.150:1.8: viññāṇe sati viññāṇaṁ upādāya uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn22.150:1.12: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vā taṁ sukhaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn22.150:1.14: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vipariṇāmadhammaṁ, api nu taṁ anupādāya uppajjeyya ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhan”ti?
root_text.sn22.150:1.21: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vā taṁ sukhaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn22.150:1.23: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vipariṇāmadhammaṁ, api nu taṁ anupādāya uppajjeyya ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhan”ti?

translation_text.sn22.150:1.2: “Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what, do pleasure and pain arise in oneself?”
translation_text.sn22.150:1.3: “Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …”
translation_text.sn22.150:1.4: “When form exists, because of grasping form, pleasure and pain arise in oneself.
translation_text.sn22.150:1.8: consciousness exists, because of grasping consciousness, pleasure and pain arise in oneself.
translation_text.sn22.150:1.12: “But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?”
translation_text.sn22.150:1.14: “But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would pleasure and pain arise in oneself?”
translation_text.sn22.150:1.21: “But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?”
translation_text.sn22.150:1.23: “But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would pleasure and pain arise in oneself?”
translation_text.sn22.150:1.26: They understand: ‘… there is nothing further for this place.’”
